name: Fujairah
history: Fujairah is the only emirate in the United Arab Emirates located on the eastern coast of the Musandam Peninsula, giving it a distinct geographic and historical identity separate from its western neighbors. The region has been an important maritime trading hub for centuries, with evidence of settlement dating back to ancient times when it served as a crucial port along Indian Ocean trade routes. During the 18th and 19th centuries, Fujairah was largely autonomous, resisting the influence of major regional powers until it formally joined the UAE federation in 1971 as the seventh emirate. The discovery of oil in the 1980s transformed the emirate's economy, though it remains less developed and more traditionally oriented than its wealthier counterparts.
culturalNotes: Fujairah maintains a more conservative and traditional character than many other Emirates, with strong ties to its Bedouin and maritime heritage that remain evident in local customs and daily life. The emirate is known for its natural beauty, including dramatic mountainous terrain, pristine beaches, and the absence of the intensive urban development found in Dubai and Abu Dhabi, making it a destination for those seeking authentic Emirati culture. Traditional practices such as pearl diving, fishing, and falconry continue to hold cultural significance here, and the population takes pride in preserving these heritage traditions.
bestTimeToVisit: Visit from November to March for pleasant weather and excellent diving and snorkeling conditions.
languageNotes: Arabic, particularly the Gulf dialect, is the primary language spoken in Fujairah, though English is increasingly common in business and tourism sectors. Useful phrases include "As-salamu alaikum" (peace be upon you) for greetings and "Shukran" (thank you), which are deeply appreciated by locals as signs of respect for their culture.

places.name: Fujairah City
places.description: The capital and main hub of Fujairah emirate, offering a blend of modern amenities and traditional Emirati culture along the Arabian Gulf coast. It features the impressive Fujairah Fort, bustling souks, and waterfront promenades that showcase the region's heritage and contemporary development.

activities.detail: Explore this 16th-century fortification perched on a hilltop overlooking the city, featuring traditional architecture, museum exhibits, and panoramic views of the Arabian Gulf coastline.
Stroll along this waterfront promenade lined with landscaped gardens, modern sculptures, and sea views, perfect for evening walks and sunset viewing.
Wander through the traditional marketplace where locals shop for spices, textiles, handicrafts, and fresh produce in narrow alleyways filled with authentic Emirati commerce.
Visit this compact museum displaying Emirati heritage artifacts, archaeological finds, traditional weapons, and exhibits documenting the emirate's maritime and cultural history.
Observe this striking modern architectural blend located near the waterfront, featuring a distinctive design that honors Islamic traditions while embracing contemporary aesthetics.
Relax on the sandy Gulf beach where calm waters offer swimming and water sports, with facilities including cafes and shaded areas for families.

food.detail: A slow-cooked savory dish made from wheat, meat (usually lamb or chicken), and butter, traditionally prepared during Ramadan and served as a hearty main course.
Locally-caught Gulf grouper prepared grilled or fried with aromatic spices, lemon, and served whole or filleted at waterfront restaurants near the corniche.
A fragrant rice dish cooked with meat, tomatoes, and Gulf spices including cardamom and cloves, reflecting the region's seafaring spice trade heritage.
Traditional sweet fried dough balls drizzled with date syrup and dusted with sesame seeds, commonly found in the souk and served as an evening treat.
The quintessential hospitality experience featuring thick Arabic coffee infused with cardamom, served in small cups alongside fresh or stuffed dates at social gatherings.

culture.detail: Wear modest clothing covering shoulders and knees, especially when visiting mosques or traditional markets; loose, lightweight fabrics are practical in the hot climate.
Greet locals with 'Assalamu alaikum' (peace be upon you) and accept hospitality graciously; always use your right hand for eating and greeting as the left is considered impolite.
Always ask permission before photographing people, particularly women, and avoid photographing government buildings or military installations.
During Ramadan, respect fasting Muslims by avoiding eating, drinking, or smoking in public during daylight hours, and expect modified business hours and quieter evening activities.

places.name: Fujairah Fort
places.description: One of the oldest and most well-preserved forts in the UAE, dating back to the 16th century and recently restored to museum standard. The fort offers panoramic views of the city and coast while providing insights into the region's defensive history and traditional architecture.

activities.detail: Tour the restored interior galleries displaying weapons, traditional dress, manuscripts, and artifacts documenting the fort's military and domestic history from the 16th century onward.
Ascend the defensive towers at each corner of the fort to experience authentic arrow slits and gun placements while enjoying elevated views of Fujairah city and the Arabian Gulf.
Capture sweeping vistas of the coastline, Fujairah beaches, and surrounding mountains from the fort's ramparts and courtyards at sunset.
Examine the restored mud-brick walls, coral stone foundations, wooden beams, and wind towers that showcase traditional Emirati building techniques and climate adaptation.
Check the fort's schedule for guided talks on regional defense strategies, Fujairah's trading history, and the lives of 16th-century garrison soldiers.
Browse locally made handicrafts, traditional pottery, and historical publications about the fort and Fujairah's maritime heritage.

food.detail: A slow-cooked wheat and meat stew traditionally prepared during Ramadan, often found at nearby restaurants and reflecting Emirati comfort food traditions.
Fresh Gulf grouper grilled with lemon and spices, a signature dish in Fujairah due to the emirate's strong fishing heritage.
Fragrant rice cooked with meat or seafood and aromatic spices like cardamom and saffron, a staple dish throughout the UAE.
Sweet fried dough balls drizzled with date syrup and sprinkled with sesame seeds, commonly sold at nearby cafés and snack vendors.
Explore beachfront restaurants near the fort serving catch-of-the-day prepared grilled or in traditional fish curries.

culture.detail: Wear modest clothing covering shoulders and knees; while the fort is a museum, it remains a significant cultural and religious site to locals.
Be prepared to remove footwear when entering prayer rooms or restored residential chambers within the fort, following local custom.
Always request permission before photographing local guides, staff, or any individuals at the fort, as this reflects respect for privacy and cultural values.
A simple 'As-salaam alaikum' (peace be upon you) and 'Shukran' (thank you) are appreciated and show respect to staff and guards.

places.name: Al Hajar Mountains
places.description: A dramatic mountain range that forms the backbone of Fujairah emirate, offering stunning scenery, hiking trails, and opportunities to explore natural wadis and valleys. The rugged terrain provides relief from coastal areas and showcases the UAE's diverse natural landscapes.

activities.detail: Trek through this spectacular wadi canyon with towering red rock formations and seasonal water pools, offering moderate trails suitable for most fitness levels.
Climb to this prominent peak that provides panoramic views across Fujairah and the surrounding mountain range, with archaeological sites of ancient settlements visible along the route.
Navigate this narrow, dramatic wadi with sheer cliff walls and hidden natural pools, popular for both hiking and photography.
Challenge yourself on the dramatic limestone and granite faces with guided climbing routes suitable for beginners to experienced climbers.

activities.text: Visit Dibba Town at Mountain Base
activities.detail: Explore the historic coastal settlement nestled where Al Hajar meets the Gulf of Oman, known for traditional fishing heritage and old-style souks.
Ride through rugged terrain and traditional village paths that wind through the mountains, offering varied difficulty levels and authentic Emirati landscape views.
Watch dawn break over the mountain range from this eastern viewpoint, with views stretching to the Arabian Gulf and neighboring Musandam Peninsula.

food.detail: Slow-cooked lamb or goat wrapped in herbs and cooked underground in traditional Bedouin style, served with rice and vegetables at mountain lodges and local restaurants.
A fragrant stew combining fresh Gulf fish with tomatoes, onions, and traditional spices, reflecting Fujairah's coastal-mountain culture.
Sample locally-produced honey from mountain apiaries and date pastes, traditionally served with fresh bread and Arabic coffee at guesthouses.
A hearty Emirati breakfast dish of pounded wheat mixed with ghee and slow-cooked meat, traditionally eaten during Ramadan and special occasions.

food.text: Fresh Mountain Spring Water and Herbal Teas
food.detail: Drink from natural springs throughout the wadis and enjoy traditional mint and medicinal herb teas prepared by local guides.

culture.detail: Many mountain areas include traditional Emirati settlements; always ask permission before photographing people or homes, and dress modestly when passing through inhabited areas.

culture.text: Bring Plenty of Water and Sun Protection
culture.detail: The mountains offer little shade and dehydration is a serious risk; carry at least 3 liters of water per person and use high-SPF sunscreen, even in winter months.
Engaging certified local guides not only ensures safer navigation of unmarked trails but also supports the community and provides authentic cultural insights.

culture.text: Best Visiting Season is October to April
culture.detail: Summer temperatures exceed 45°C making hiking dangerous; visit during cooler months when trails are safe and local communities are more actively engaged in outdoor activities.

places.name: Al Qurayyah Beach
places.description: A pristine sandy beach north of Fujairah City known for its calm waters and relatively uncrowded atmosphere compared to other emirates. The beach is ideal for swimming, water sports, and relaxing while enjoying views of the northern coast and nearby mountains.

activities.detail: The shallow, calm waters of Al Qurayyah are ideal for safe swimming and paddling, making it suitable for families and non-strong swimmers.
Explore the modest coral and marine life in the shallow offshore areas, particularly in the early morning when visibility is best.
Walk along the sandy shore to find shells, sea glass, and other natural treasures washed up by the Gulf tides.
Capture stunning sunset photos with views of the northern coast mountains and minimal light pollution interference.
Pack a meal and enjoy dining on the sand with the sea breeze, taking advantage of the beach's relatively uncrowded nature.
Rent equipment and paddle out on the calm waters to explore the coastline and enjoy a peaceful water-based activity.

food.detail: Fresh local grouper grilled whole or filleted, typically seasoned with simple spices and served at beachfront restaurants or casual eateries near Fujairah City.
A fragrant spiced rice dish layered with meat or fish, reflecting Emirati coastal culinary traditions and available at local restaurants.
Sweet fried dough balls drizzled with date molasses, a popular Emirati dessert sold at small vendors and cafes near the beach.
Platters of grilled shrimp, fish, and octopus accompanied by hummus, tabbouleh, and fresh pita bread reflecting Gulf coastal dining culture.
A traditional cooling drink served chilled, perfect for refreshment after time in the sun and widely available at local cafes.

culture.detail: Many local businesses and restaurants close or reduce service during the five daily prayer times, particularly at midday and evening.
While swimwear is acceptable on the beach, wear a cover-up when walking to and from the water or visiting nearby shops and cafes to respect local customs.
Al Qurayyah is maintained as a pristine natural space; dispose of all waste properly and avoid leaving trash to help preserve the beach for locals and future visitors.

culture.text: Cash Still Preferred in Some Areas
culture.detail: While card payments are becoming more common, small vendors and family-run eateries near the beach may prefer cash, so carry some UAE dirhams.

places.name: Wadi Wurayah
places.description: A protected natural area and UNESCO biosphere reserve featuring a picturesque wadi with freshwater pools, lush vegetation, and scenic hiking trails through rocky canyon landscapes. It offers one of the most rewarding nature experiences in the UAE with opportunities to see wildlife and enjoy waterfalls.

activities.detail: Trek through the protected canyon following the wadi bed past freshwater pools and lush date palms to reach the scenic waterfall at the valley's end.
Cool off in the natural emerald pools fed by underground springs, a refreshing contrast to the surrounding desert landscape.
Look for Arabian tahr, rock hyraxes, and various bird species endemic to the UAE's northern mountains during early morning hikes.
Capture the dramatic cascading waterfall surrounded by towering sandstone cliffs, particularly stunning after seasonal rainfall.
Navigate natural rock formations and climb through narrow canyon sections for a more adventurous experience beyond the main trail.
Ascend to elevated vantage points overlooking the wadi system where golden light illuminates the layered rock formations at dusk.

food.detail: A traditional Emirati slow-cooked meat and wheat dish commonly prepared in mountain communities and served during special occasions.
Drink directly from the natural springs in the wadi or refill bottles with the cool, mineral-rich water unique to this biosphere reserve.
Sample fresh or dried dates harvested from the date palms thriving in the wadi's microclimate, available from nearby Fujairah markets.
A fragrant Emirati rice and meat dish with spices, widely available in Fujairah town as a hearty option before or after hiking.

culture.detail: Stay on marked trails, do not remove plants or rocks, and observe all UNESCO protection regulations to preserve this delicate ecosystem.
Wear loose, covered clothing suitable for both sun protection and cultural respect, along with sturdy hiking boots for rocky terrain.
Pack at least 2 liters of water per person as there are limited facilities, and inform someone of your hiking plans before departure.
Plan visits between October and April when temperatures are moderate; summer heat makes hiking dangerous in this exposed canyon environment.

places.name: Khor Fakkan
places.description: A scenic coastal settlement renowned for its stunning crescent beach backed by dramatic cliffs and the iconic Khor Fakkan Mosque with its distinctive architecture. The town offers excellent beaches, a charming corniche, and serves as a base for exploring both coastal and mountain attractions.

activities.detail: Wade and swim in the sheltered crescent bay framed by limestone cliffs, ideal for families and snorkeling enthusiasts.
Admire the striking white marble mosque with its modern architectural design overlooking the corniche, open to visitors outside prayer times.
Stroll the waterfront promenade lined with palm trees, cafés, and views of the Arabian Gulf, perfect for sunset viewing.
Explore an aquarium showcasing local marine life and coral ecosystems found in the Gulf waters surrounding Khor Fakkan.
Trek the dramatic limestone ridges above the beach for panoramic vistas and photography opportunities of the bay and surrounding mountains.
Join local boat operators for deep-sea or light fishing excursions in the Gulf, with opportunities to catch grouper, snapper, and mackerel.
Enjoy landscaped gardens, shaded seating areas, and a family-friendly beach zone with facilities and water sports rentals.

food.detail: Savor locally caught grouper seasoned simply with olive oil, lemon, and spices, a Gulf coastal staple best enjoyed at beachside restaurants.
Try this fragrant Emirati rice dish layered with slow-cooked meat, aromatic spices, and often served with yogurt on the side.
Experience Indian-influenced biryani adapted with Gulf fish, served at local eateries and reflecting the region's multicultural cuisine.
Order grilled jumbo prawns charred over flame and tossed with garlic, cilantro, and traditional spice blends at waterfront dining spots.
Start your day with warm khubz bread, hummus, cheese, date jam, and strong Arabic coffee at local cafés near the corniche.
Sample a platter of small plates featuring calamari, octopus, and fish prepared with Middle Eastern seasonings and served with bread and tahini.

culture.detail: Plan visits to public spaces and beaches around the five daily prayer times; many businesses close temporarily during Maghrib (sunset prayer).
While swimwear is acceptable at the beach, modest clothing covering shoulders and knees is respectful when in town or visiting the mosque.
Emiratis value warm greetings and hospitality; a simple 'Salam alaikum' (peace be upon you) is appreciated, and accepting offered coffee or tea is customary.
Always ask permission before photographing people or entering private spaces; avoid photographing government buildings or military installations.

places.name: Wadi Hq
places.description: A scenic wadi valley in the Al Hajar Mountains offering moderate hiking trails with natural pools and traditional village settlements scattered throughout. The wadi provides an accessible yet authentic experience of mountain life and desert hydrology in the emirate.

activities.detail: Trek through the wadi bed to discover cool freshwater pools fed by seasonal mountain streams, ideal for swimming and cooling off after the hike.
Explore scattered Emirati mountain hamlets with traditional stone houses and terraced gardens still maintained by local families.
Climb to elevated vantage points along the wadi rim for panoramic views of stacked rock formations and the valley floor below.
Capture the golden light illuminating the canyon walls and villages during early morning hours when shadows create dramatic contrasts.
Visit cascading falls that appear after winter rains, flowing down cliff faces into deeper pools suitable for adventurous swimmers.
Examine exposed geological layers and distinctive striations in the Al Hajar Mountains that reveal millions of years of tectonic activity.
Learn about native mountain vegetation including frankincense, ghaf trees, and medicinal herbs adapted to the arid environment.

food.detail: A slow-cooked meat dish using locally herded goat meat seasoned with traditional spices, served with flatbread and consumed in village homes.
Sweet treats made with dates from lower elevations and mountain bee honey, often shared by residents as hospitality offerings.
Fresh-caught mountain fish prepared simply over open fires by villagers, flavored only with salt and herbs growing nearby.
Strong, lightly spiced coffee served in traditional dallah pots, an essential part of welcoming visitors to the wadi communities.
Traditional flatbread baked in outdoor ovens using barley flour, a staple that has sustained mountain communities for generations.

culture.detail: Always request consent from villagers before taking photos, as many residents value privacy and traditional customs regarding image-making.
Be mindful that locals may pause activities for prayer at dawn, midday, and afternoon; plan hikes accordingly and observe quiet respect during these times.
Wear long pants and covered shoulders when passing through inhabited settlements out of respect for conservative Islamic and traditional mountain customs.
Use 'As-salamu alaikum' (peace be upon you) when encountering residents, as this respectful greeting is essential to community interaction in Emirati mountain villages.

places.name: Fujairah Beach Park
places.description: A well-maintained public beach park in Fujairah City offering sandy shores, picnic facilities, and recreational amenities for families and visitors. The park provides direct access to the Arabian Gulf with a relaxed atmosphere and is a popular spot for sunset watching.

activities.detail: Enjoy the calm waters of the Arabian Gulf with designated swimming areas that are generally safe and suitable for families.
Watch the sun descend over the Gulf from the beach's western vantage point, a popular evening activity for photographers and couples.
Use the park's provided picnic tables and shaded areas to enjoy meals with family while overlooking the shoreline.
Stroll or run along the sandy shore early morning or late afternoon when temperatures are more comfortable.
Let children use the dedicated playground equipment and shallow beach areas designed for young visitors.
Capture images of the waterfront, traditional fishing boats, and coastal landscape that characterize Fujairah's maritime heritage.

food.detail: Fresh Gulf grouper seasoned simply with lemon and spices, commonly served at beachside cafés and restaurants near the park.
A traditional Emirati spiced rice dish cooked with meat or seafood, often available at nearby food vendors and casual dining spots.
Platters of grilled shrimp, crab, and fish paired with hummus, tabbouleh, and flatbread served at waterfront establishments.
Sweet fried dough balls drizzled with date syrup and sprinkled with sesame seeds, a popular dessert available from nearby kiosks.
Spiced meat wrapped in pita bread with garlic sauce and vegetables, widely available from casual food stands around the park.

culture.detail: Wear modest swimwear and cover-ups when not in the water, as Fujairah is a more conservative emirate than some coastal areas.
Expect the park to be busiest on Friday mornings and afternoons when local families gather; arrive early for parking and facilities.
During Ramadan, eating, drinking, and smoking in public are discouraged during daylight hours, and beach activities may be quieter until evening.
Always ask permission before photographing individuals or groups, particularly families and women, as privacy is valued in Emirati culture.

places.name: Masafi
places.description: A traditional mountain village famous for its pottery workshops and artisanal handicrafts preserved through generations of craftsmen. Visitors can watch potters at work, purchase authentic ceramic pieces, and experience traditional Emirati mountain village culture.

activities.detail: Visit active ceramic studios where master potters demonstrate traditional hand-throwing and wheel-building techniques passed down through generations.
Browse and purchase authentic handcrafted ceramic pieces including water vessels, decorative plates, and traditional cooking pots directly from artisans.
Explore the narrow lanes and traditional stone houses of the village to experience authentic Emirati mountain culture and architecture.
Participate in hands-on clay-working sessions where visitors can learn basic pottery techniques and create their own ceramic piece to take home.
Visit the natural freshwater spring that has served the village for centuries and is now bottled as Masafi mineral water, a local product.

food.detail: A fragrant rice dish cooked with slow-stewed meat, traditionally prepared in mountain villages and served with yogurt and pickles.
A slow-cooked porridge-like dish made from wheat, meat, and ghee that is a staple comfort food in Emirati mountain communities.
Local bakeries offer traditional flatbreads often served with wild herbs, cheese, and olive oil gathered from the surrounding Hajar Mountains.
A rich dessert made with phyllo pastry, milk, coconut, and nuts that is popular at gatherings in traditional Emirati villages.
Locally-raised lamb grilled with traditional spices and herbs, reflecting the village's pastoral heritage and mountain cuisine.

culture.detail: Potters typically work during morning and early afternoon hours; visit between 8am and 2pm to see artisans at their wheels.
Always ask permission before photographing artisans or their workshops, as many craftspeople appreciate being consulted first.
Wear respectful clothing covering shoulders and knees when visiting the village and entering workshops out of cultural respect.
Purchasing directly from potters and workshops supports families and ensures authentic pieces while learning their personal stories.

places.name: Al Aqah Beach
places.description: A sheltered sandy beach popular for water sports and swimming, offering calm waters protected by surrounding geography. The beach is well-equipped with facilities and serves as a base for exploring both the coast and nearby mountain attractions.

activities.detail: The beach is sheltered by rocky outcrops and geography, making it ideal for safe swimming year-round with minimal currents.
Explore nearby coral reefs and marine life in the clear shallow waters, with several dive operators based at the beach.
Rent water sports equipment to paddle along the protected coastline and explore hidden coves and rock formations.
The well-equipped beach facilities include courts and equipment rental for volleyball, jet skis, and other recreational activities.
The western orientation and surrounding mountains create dramatic golden-hour photography opportunities and peaceful evening atmosphere.
Access nearby Hajar Mountain trails and wadis from the beach area for trekking and exploration of inland terrain.

food.detail: Fresh local grouper fish grilled with simple seasonings, a staple at beachside restaurants along the Fujairah coast.
Traditional Emirati rice dish cooked with meat or seafood, fragrant spices, and vegetables, commonly served at local eateries.
Assorted grilled fish, calamari, and shrimp served with hummus, tabbouleh, and flatbread at waterfront establishments.
Warm, creamy Middle Eastern bread pudding dessert made with milk, nuts, and raisins, widely available at local restaurants.

food.text: Fresh fish with Emirati spice blends
food.detail: Whole fish seasoned with local zaatar, sumac, and chili, then grilled or baked at beachfront dining venues.

culture.detail: While Al Aqah is more relaxed than some beaches, women should wear modest swimwear and men should avoid swimming shirtless in public areas.
Most beach facilities and nearby restaurants may have reduced hours during the five daily prayer times, particularly Jumu'ah (Friday prayers).
While card payments are available, cash in AED is preferred by many small beachside shops and informal food vendors.

places.name: Fujairah Central Souk
places.description: A traditional marketplace in the heart of Fujairah City offering local produce, spices, textiles, and handicrafts reflecting Emirati trading heritage. The souk provides an authentic shopping experience and insight into daily local life away from modern malls.

activities.detail: Walk through aromatic sections selling saffron, cardamom, cloves, and locally-sourced spice blends used in Emirati cooking.
Examine traditional fabrics, embroidered textiles, and colorful cloths sold by merchants who can advise on quality and authenticity.
Visit the vegetable and fruit sections to see locally-grown dates, seasonal produce, and items you won't find in supermarkets.
Discover handmade pottery, woven baskets, traditional jewelry, and locally-crafted items reflecting Emirati artisanship.
Engage shopkeepers in conversation to learn about products, haggling customs, and stories of the souk's trading heritage.
Arrive before 9 AM when the souk is most bustling with vendors setting up, locals shopping, and the atmosphere most authentic.

food.detail: Sample seasonal Gulf dates sold loose or in blocks, often paired with strong Arabic coffee, reflecting the region's primary agricultural export.
Seek out simple grilled fish prepared fresh at small food stalls, a staple reflecting Fujairah's coastal fishing heritage.
Try these golden fried balls dusted with powdered sugar and drizzled with date syrup or honey, a beloved Emirati dessert sold at small food vendors.
Purchase savory wraps from modest food stalls serving slow-cooked meat, hummus, and pickles in flatbread, popular quick meals for souk shoppers.
Enjoy complimentary small cups of traditional qahwa offered by vendors or at dedicated coffee corners, an essential hospitality custom.

culture.detail: Negotiate prices respectfully with vendors, especially on textiles and handicrafts, as it is customary practice and part of the souk experience.
Wear loose, covered clothing out of respect for the traditional setting and local customs; women should especially avoid very short or revealing garments.
Learning basic greetings like 'As-salaam alaikum' (peace be upon you) and 'Shukran' (thank you) builds rapport with vendors and shows cultural respect.
Always ask permission before photographing vendors or their goods, as some locals prefer not to be photographed for cultural or religious reasons.

restaurants.name: Al Reef Bakery & Restaurant
restaurants.description: Family-friendly establishment serving authentic Emirati dishes and freshly baked bread in a casual setting.

restaurants.name: Al Dafrah Restaurant
restaurants.description: Long-established restaurant renowned for traditional Emirati cuisine and generous portion sizes.

restaurants.name: Al Zarooni Restaurant
restaurants.description: Popular Lebanese grill specializing in charcoal-grilled meats and mezze platters with a warm ambiance.

restaurants.name: Sea Palace Restaurant
restaurants.description: Upscale seafood restaurant offering fresh catches with panoramic sea views from the dining area.

restaurants.name: Al Safa Restaurant
restaurants.description: Traditional Arabic restaurant featuring grilled meats, fresh salads, and regional specialties in a cozy environment.

restaurants.name: Ravi Restaurant
restaurants.description: Casual Pakistani eatery popular with locals, serving authentic curries and tandoori dishes at excellent value.

restaurants.name: Al Manara Restaurant
restaurants.description: Beachfront seafood restaurant in Dibba known for fresh fish preparations and traditional Gulf cooking methods.

restaurants.name: Al Islami Restaurant
restaurants.description: Simple, authentic Emirati restaurant frequented by families serving traditional dishes and fresh juices.

restaurants.name: Coast Restaurant
restaurants.description: Contemporary restaurant with coastal views serving international cuisine and fresh seafood specialties.

restaurants.name: Al Arish Restaurant
restaurants.description: Established restaurant offering traditional Emirati breakfast and lunch featuring slow-cooked heritage recipes.

restaurants.name: Khan Al Khaleej Restaurant
restaurants.description: Gulf cuisine specialist featuring regional dishes from across the Arabian Peninsula in a traditional setting.

restaurants.name: Al Reef Restaurant & Cafe
restaurants.description: Casual beachfront cafe serving Arabic grills, fresh juices, and light meals with relaxed atmosphere.

restaurants.name: Al Khayma Restaurant
restaurants.description: Traditional Bedouin-style restaurant offering heritage Emirati cuisine in an authentic tent-inspired setting.

restaurants.name: Marina Restaurant
restaurants.description: Contemporary seafood dining venue near the port featuring daily fresh catches and Mediterranean preparations.
